cataloging_subject.label: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The distinct anonymous Middle English Egerton Version of the Book of John Mandeville, uniquely attested in British Library Egerton MS 1982. This record does not describe the abstract multilingual Book of John Mandeville, the physical codex as a whole, the Cotton Version, Warner's bilingual 1889 volume, the University of Michigan selection, or the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-redactor; traditionally attributed to the fictional or legendary narrator John Mandeville
catalog_summary.date: Around 1400; uniquely attested in Egerton MS 1982
catalog_summary.region: England; the unique witness's scribal language is localized to the North Riding of Yorkshire

titles.value: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels
Here begynnez þe buke of John Maundeuill
notes: The manuscript heading is preserved in Warner's transcription and the U-M encoding; capitalization is normalized here from display capitals.
Short title of Warner's 1889 edition; the edition's long subtitle also calls the text a hitherto unpublished English version from Egerton MS 1982.

agents.name: John Mandeville
notes: Narrating persona and traditional author attribution. Current scholarship treats the identity as unresolved and commonly understands the Book as an anonymous compilation rather than an eyewitness account by a securely historical knight.

agents.name: George F. Warner
notes: Editor, transcriber, collator, and commentator of the 1889 Roxburghe Club edition used by U-M.
The Egerton Version combines material associated with the Defective tradition and the Anglo-Latin Royal tradition. No reviewed source identifies the Middle English translator-redactor.
Adaptation distinguishes the Egerton Version's conflation and rearrangement from a simple word-for-word translation claim.
The prologue names an English knight from St Albans as first-person narrator, but the persona is legendary or pseudonymous and does not establish a historical author's identity.

scope.description: Copying of the unique surviving Egerton witness

notes: Witness-level copying is kept distinct from responsibility for creating the intellectual version.

scope.description: Egerton-base English text and Harley/Royal-base French parallel text
Cotton English variants and supplies and French-witness apparatus
Introduction, footnotes, glossary, commentary, and index in the 1889 edition

notes: The unique witness is dated around 1400 in modern scholarship. That date constrains the attested version but does not prove that translation, redaction, and copying were one event.
The Egerton Version is uniquely attested in this witness.

The Egerton prologue says the narrator departed in 1332; the resulting narrated travel span is not a composition date or verified biography. French, Latin, Cotton, and several other versions instead give 1322, and Warner's long title uses 1322-1356.
This is an English-language version in the English manuscript tradition; no precise place of redaction is established.

scope.description: Scribal language of the unique Egerton witness

notes: LALME/MEG-C localizes the witness sample to the North Riding. This is not treated as proof that the translator-redactor worked there.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header omits the primary language; direct inspection of the encoded work establishes Middle English.

scope.description: Quotations, titles, formulas, and individual expressions embedded in the Middle English prose

language_statements.label: Ancient Greek

scope.description: A small number of transliterated quotations and an alphabet passage whose Greek glyphs are absent from the XML

notes: Thirty-four encoded Greek FOREIGN nodes contain GAP rather than glyphs; four other Greek-language quotations are transliterated. Warner's French parallel is an edition layer and is not a co-primary language of this Middle English version.

form_statements.description: A continuous prose compilation with embedded quotations. The thirty-four numbered chapters in Warner and U-M are editorial divisions imposed on an undivided manuscript.

genre_statements.label: Travel narrative
notes: The first-person itinerary is a literary compilation and should not be cataloged as verified eyewitness travel.

subject_statements.label: Routes to Jerusalem, sacred geography, pilgrimage sites, and relics
The Eastern Mediterranean, Near East, Central Asia, and India
Christianities, Islam, Judaism, other religions, and reported customs
notes: Descriptions reflect medieval Christian polemic and inherited literary sources, not neutral ethnography.

subject_statements.label: Marvelous peoples, animals, islands, and natural wonders
Cosmography, the round earth, geography, and antipodes
The Great Khan, Prester John, and imagined Eastern polities

summaries.value: A northern Middle English version of the Book of John Mandeville, framed as the recollections and guidance of an English knight who claims to have travelled from St Albans through the Holy Land and onward across Asia. The work combines pilgrimage routes and sacred sites with geography, religions, political powers, natural history, cosmography, and marvels. It is an anonymous literary compilation and adaptation of inherited authorities and version traditions, not reliable evidence of the narrator's claimed eyewitness journey.

The unique Egerton manuscript has no chapter divisions. Warner divided its English text into thirty-four chapters using Cotton headings closely related to the French Harley and Royal witnesses, printed a French source text in parallel, and added extensive apparatus and commentary. U-M selected Warner's English text and footnotes, omitted the entire French stream and front and back matter, linked its editorial divisions to the Cotton encoding through CORRESP, and inserted into the body an Egerton-only passage that Warner had placed in commentary.

extent.description: One continuous prose work. The U-M representation contains a prologue and thirty-four editor-imposed chapter DIV1 elements, 214 body paragraphs, print-page milestones for Warner pp. 1-156, and intended folio-side milestones spanning approximately Egerton fols. 3r-132v. Its 580 NOTE1 elements and 56 SUPPLIED elements are nested editorial apparatus or restorations and are excluded from structural counts. The thirty-four chapters are not intrinsic manuscript sections.

content_parts.label: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels
content_parts.summary: The complete continuous Egerton-version prose, from its pilgrimage prologue through routes to Jerusalem, descriptions of Asia and marvels, cosmographical reflections, and the narrator's closing appeal for prayer.

locators.value: English text pp. 1-156; one Egerton-only passage printed in commentary p. 220

notes: Warner's and U-M's chapter divisions are useful access points but do not warrant thirty-four intrinsic content parts in a work-level manifest.

related_works.title: The Book of John Mandeville
notes: The Book is a multilingual and multiversion textual tradition; this record catalogs only its Egerton Middle English version.

related_works.title: The Cotton Version of Mandeville's Travels
notes: Warner used Cotton Titus C XVI for chapter headings, explicit supplied passages, and variants. U-M added chapter-level links to its Cotton encoding; this does not make the Cotton Version the base text of the Egerton Version.

related_works.title: The Defective Version of the Book of John Mandeville
notes: Seymour describes one lost source strand behind the Egerton Version as a manuscript of the Defective Version.

related_works.title: The Anglo-Latin Royal Version of the Book of John Mandeville
notes: Seymour describes the second source strand as a lost Middle English translation of the Anglo-Latin Royal Version, not direct copying from an identified surviving Latin manuscript.

evidence.summary: Identifies U-M item acd9576, Warner's 1889 source, the omitted French and front/back matter, Cotton CORRESP pointers, embedded provider terms, and the selected Egerton English text with editorial apparatus.

evidence.citation: Element and attribute inspection of General Corpus acd9576.xml.
evidence.locator: BODY element counts, DIV1 identifiers and CORRESP values, language-bearing nodes, notes, and supplied text
evidence.summary: Finds 35 top-level prose divisions (one prologue and thirty-four editorial chapters), 214 body paragraphs, 156 print-page markers, 257 intended folio markers, 580 notes, 56 supplied spans, and chapter-level Cotton links. It also distinguishes embedded Latin and Greek from the omitted French edition stream and identifies the anomalous Eg.Ch.31 to Cot.Ch.30 mapping.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Mandeville's Travels: The Egerton Version, item acd9576.
evidence.locator: Item information, rights statement, and table of contents
evidence.summary: Confirms the public 2003 digital item, stable handle, Warner source edition, thirty-four displayed chapters, and current Public Domain / No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: George F. Warner, ed., The Buke of John Maundeuill, Roxburghe Club Publications 119 (Westminster: Nichols and Sons, 1889), Internet Archive thebukeofjohnmaundeuill.
evidence.locator: Title page; introduction pp. v-xlvi; English and French parallel text pp. 1-156; commentary pp. 157-220; index through p. 232; twenty-eight plates
evidence.summary: The inspected complete scan establishes Warner's Egerton-base English transcription, Cotton headings/supplies/variants, Harley and Royal French base text, Sloane and Grenville French apparatus, manuscript and edition structure, the relocation of an Egerton-only passage to commentary p. 220, and twenty-eight plates from Additional MS 24189.

evidence.citation: Folger Shakespeare Library catalog, The Buke of John Maundeuill, record 37371.
evidence.summary: Confirms the 1889 Westminster imprint, Roxburghe Club series, physical extent, large-paper format, and twenty-eight reproduced miniatures from British Library Additional MS 24189.
notes: The catalog's older Jean d'Outremeuse authorship heading is not adopted as established authorship.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Egerton MS 1982.
evidence.summary: Confirms the unique vellum manuscript's shelfmark, fifteenth-century date range, English language, legendary-author cataloging, physical availability, and absence of current digitized content.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Cotton MS Titus C XVI.
evidence.summary: Identifies the unique Cotton Version witness as an English manuscript of the first quarter of the fifteenth century, with 133 extant folios and no current public digital content.

evidence.citation: M. C. Seymour, ed., The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels, Early English Text Society Original Series 336 (Oxford University Press, 2010), Folger record 263094.
evidence.summary: Confirms a unique manuscript written around 1400 and summarizes Seymour's reconstruction of the version as conflating a lost Defective-Version manuscript and a lost Middle English translation of the Anglo-Latin Royal Version.

evidence.citation: The Book of John Mandeville, scholarly introduction, Middle English Texts Series Editions.
evidence.summary: Synthesizes current scholarship on the anonymous or pseudonymous compiler, debated French origin and date, major sources, multiversion transmission, the unique Cotton and Egerton Middle English versions, and the work's travel, pilgrimage, geographical, and marvels genres.

evidence.citation: A Linguistic Atlas of Early Middle English and Middle English Grammar Corpus, manuscript sample L0457 for British Library Egerton MS 1982.
evidence.summary: The scholarly corpus index dates the sample to the turn of the fifteenth century and localizes its scribal language to the North Riding of Yorkshire.
notes: The item-level PDF at the official University of Stavanger URL was indexed and discoverable but returned HTTP 403 to automated retrieval; it was not treated as a verified downloadable deliverable or cached.

open_questions.question: When and where was the Egerton Version created, and which distinctive readings belong to its translator-redactor rather than the sole surviving scribe?
next_steps: Consult Seymour's full 2010 introduction and subsequent version scholarship.
Keep the c. 1400 witness date and North Riding scribal localization separate from an unproven redaction date and place.

open_questions.question: Can the two lost source strands posited for the Egerton Version be related more precisely to extant Defective and Anglo-Latin Royal witnesses?
next_steps: Review Seymour's full textual apparatus and later stemmatic research.
Retain the lost intermediaries as reported or hypothesized entities, not identified physical exemplars.

open_questions.question: What is the textual status and origin of the Egerton-only Thule and Saint Thomas of Canterbury passage that Warner removed to commentary and U-M restored to the body?
next_steps: Compare the passage in Seymour's edition and apparatus with the Egerton manuscript and wider Mandeville traditions.
Preserve Warner's and U-M's different placements in lineage rather than selecting one silently.

notes: The U-M header lists Latin and Greek but omits primary Middle English; language statements were derived from direct text inspection rather than copied mechanically from the header.

Warner's French parallel is a source-edition and apparatus layer. Because U-M explicitly omitted it, French is not treated as a language of the cataloged Egerton Middle English work or its selected digital text.
The manuscript's lack of divisions prevents Warner's thirty-four Cotton-derived chapter headings from being counted as intrinsic work structure.
Copyright, access, local source-cache delivery, and the conflict between U-M's current public-domain statement and its embedded redistribution restriction are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.
